title: NASA Satellite Map Shows Stifling Heat Dome Squatting Over the United States
gist: After a heat dome hit the East Coast around July 4, NASA satellite data from the Goddard Earth Observing System shows a second heat dome baking the central United States as of July 12, 2026, with modeled temperatures exceeding roughly 110°F in some regions.

insights.claim: Heat domes form when high-pressure air masses suppress convection and cloud formation, trapping heat over a breeze-less area; they develop slowly, tend to linger, and become especially dangerous when combined with high humidity, producing a 'steam-cooker' effect.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: A recurring, persistent (not transient) extreme-weather mechanism relevant to grid-demand, agriculture, and public-health risk modeling during summer months.
insights.locator: These weather systems occur when masses of air

insights.claim: A heat dome struck the U.S. East Coast around the July 4 holiday and has since passed, but a separate heat dome is now baking the central United States as of mid-July 2026.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Sequential regional heat domes signal a summer pattern of recurring extreme-heat events across different U.S. regions, relevant to energy demand and crop-stress forecasting.
insights.locator: While the eastern July 4 heat dome has now passed

insights.claim: NASA's Goddard Earth Observing System produced a modeled temperature image of the continental U.S. from July 12, 2026, showing plains states blanketed in oppressive heat with modeled temperatures soaring above an estimated 110°F in some regions.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Concrete, dated extreme-heat data point (110°F+ in plains states, July 12, 2026) usable for tracking summer weather-driven demand shocks (power, water, agriculture).
insights.locator: NASA recently released an image of temperatures
